title: The Old Lighthouse Keeper
summary: An elderly Cornish lighthouse keeper looks back on forty winters watching the same coast and reflects on the strange comfort of routine.
body: My father was a lighthouse keeper, and so was I for forty winters. The lighthouse stands on a thin rock at the end of a long bay in Cornwall. When I was young, I thought the job would be lonely. It can be, but not in the way most people think. The light needs you every night. The wind tells you when to fix the door. The gulls remind you that you are not the only one on the rock. I have seen storms throw boats onto the cliffs and I have seen mornings when the sea looked like a polished plate. My wife came to live with me at the lighthouse for thirty of those years. We didn't talk much, but we always made tea at the same hour. She passed away two winters ago. The new keepers are different now — most of the work is done by computers. They visit only once a week to check the lamp. I am retired now and I live in a small village nearby. From my kitchen window I can still see the light turning at night. People sometimes ask me if I miss it. I tell them I don't, because part of me never really left.

annotations.exampleEn: A lighthouse keeper lives close to the sea.
Birds rested on a thin rock above the water.
We sailed along a long bay before sunset.
I thought the trip would be lonely, but it wasn't.
The gulls flew above the boats.
Big storms can throw small boats onto the cliffs.

annotations.meaningVi: một chiếc đĩa được đánh bóng (ý chỉ mặt biển phẳng lặng)
annotations.exampleEn: After the storm, the lake looked like a polished plate.
Her grandfather passed away last spring.

annotations.meaningVi: đèn (ở đây là đèn hải đăng)
annotations.exampleEn: The lamp at the top of the lighthouse shines for miles.

annotations.phrase: part of me never really left
annotations.meaningVi: một phần trong tôi chưa bao giờ thực sự rời đi
annotations.exampleEn: She moved abroad, but part of her never really left home.

grammarNotes.title: Past simple for completed past habits and events
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì quá khứ đơn (V2) cho hành động hoặc thói quen đã kết thúc. Trong bài: 'My wife came to live with me at the lighthouse for thirty of those years.' và 'We didn't talk much, but we always made tea at the same hour.' Đây là chuỗi sự việc trong quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Past Simple for finished actions or past habits. From the text: 'My wife came to live with me…' and 'We didn't talk much, but we always made tea at the same hour.'

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life experiences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về trải nghiệm trong đời, không xác định thời gian cụ thể. Trong bài: 'I have seen storms throw boats onto the cliffs' và 'I have seen mornings when the sea looked like a polished plate.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Present Perfect (have/has + past participle) for life experiences when the exact time is not important. From the text: 'I have seen storms throw boats onto the cliffs.'

translationVi: Cha tôi từng là người canh hải đăng, và tôi cũng làm nghề ấy suốt bốn mươi mùa đông. Ngọn hải đăng đứng trên một mỏm đá hẹp ở cuối một vịnh dài thuộc Cornwall. Khi còn trẻ, tôi nghĩ công việc này sẽ rất cô đơn. Nó có thể cô đơn, nhưng không theo cách mà nhiều người tưởng. Mỗi đêm ngọn đèn cần mình. Gió báo cho mình biết khi nào phải sửa cánh cửa. Lũ mòng biển nhắc rằng mình không phải sinh vật duy nhất trên mỏm đá. Tôi đã thấy những cơn bão hất thuyền lên vách đá, và cũng thấy những buổi sáng mặt biển phẳng lặng như chiếc đĩa được đánh bóng. Vợ tôi chuyển ra hải đăng sống cùng tôi trong ba mươi năm. Chúng tôi ít nói chuyện, nhưng luôn pha trà đúng một giờ mỗi ngày. Bà ấy qua đời hai mùa đông trước. Người canh mới bây giờ khác xưa — phần lớn công việc đã do máy tính làm. Họ chỉ ghé một lần mỗi tuần để kiểm tra đèn. Giờ tôi đã nghỉ hưu và sống ở một ngôi làng nhỏ gần đó. Từ cửa sổ bếp, tôi vẫn thấy ánh đèn quay trong đêm. Người ta đôi khi hỏi tôi có nhớ nó không. Tôi nói không, vì một phần trong tôi chưa bao giờ thực sự rời đi.

questions.prompt: Who else in the narrator's family was a lighthouse keeper?
questions.explanation: 'My father was a lighthouse keeper, and so was I.'
questions.hint: Listen for a family member in the very first sentence.

questions.prompt: How many winters did the narrator work as a keeper?
questions.hint: Listen for the number of years.

options: On a thin rock at the end of a long bay
On a small island in the middle of the sea
questions.explanation: 'The lighthouse stands on a thin rock at the end of a long bay in Cornwall.'
questions.hint: Listen for the description of the lighthouse's location.

questions.prompt: What did he expect the job to be like when he was young?
questions.explanation: 'I thought the job would be lonely.'
questions.hint: Listen for the word he chose as a young man.

questions.prompt: What is one thing the gulls remind him of?
options: That he is not the only one on the rock
questions.explanation: 'The gulls remind you that you are not the only one on the rock.'
questions.hint: Listen for the message of the gulls.

questions.prompt: How long did his wife live with him at the lighthouse?
questions.explanation: 'My wife came to live with me at the lighthouse for thirty of those years.'
questions.hint: Listen for a number of years connected to his wife.

questions.prompt: What small daily ritual did he and his wife share?
options: Making tea at the same hour
questions.explanation: 'we always made tea at the same hour.'
questions.hint: Listen for a habit they did at the same time each day.

questions.prompt: What does 'the sea looked like a polished plate' suggest?
options: The sea was full of fish
The sea was completely calm and shiny
questions.explanation: A 'polished plate' is smooth and reflective, like a very calm sea.
questions.hint: Think about how a polished plate looks.

questions.prompt: How is the lighthouse run today?
options: By a small team of fishermen
Mostly by computers, checked once a week
questions.explanation: 'most of the work is done by computers. They visit only once a week to check the lamp.'
questions.hint: Listen for what does most of the work now.

questions.prompt: Where does the narrator live now?
options: In a city far from the coast
In a small village nearby, with a view of the light
On a boat in the bay
questions.explanation: 'I live in a small village nearby. From my kitchen window I can still see the light turning at night.'
questions.hint: Listen for both where he lives and what he can see.

questions.prompt: What does he mean when he says 'part of me never really left'?
options: He goes back to the lighthouse every weekend
His memories and feelings are still with the lighthouse, so he doesn't miss it the usual way
questions.explanation: He means his connection to the lighthouse is so deep that it has stayed with him emotionally.
questions.hint: Think about a feeling rather than a physical thing.

cloze.template: My father was a lighthouse keeper, and so was I for forty {{b1}}. The lighthouse stands on a thin rock at the end of a long bay in Cornwall. When I was young, I thought the job would be {{b2}}. It can be, but not in the way most people think. The light needs you every night. The wind tells you when to fix the door. The {{b3}} remind you that you are not the only one on the rock. I have seen storms throw boats onto the {{b4}} and I have seen mornings when the sea looked like a polished {{b5}}. My wife came to live with me at the lighthouse for thirty of those years. We didn't talk much, but we always made {{b6}} at the same hour. She passed {{b7}} two winters ago. The new keepers are different now — most of the work is done by computers. They visit only once a week to check the {{b8}}. I am retired now and I live in a small village nearby. From my kitchen window I can still see the light {{b9}} at night. People sometimes ask me if I miss it. I tell them I don't, because part of me never really {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'winters' — he uses winters to count his years of work.
'lonely' — what he expected the work to be when he was young.
'gulls' — sea birds that share the rock with him.
'cliffs' — high rocks above the sea where storms throw boats.
'plate' — a polished plate is smooth and shiny like calm water.
'away' — 'pass away' is a polite phrase meaning to die.
'lamp' — the lighthouse lamp, which the new keepers check weekly.
'turning' — a lighthouse light rotates to flash in all directions.
'left' — 'part of me never really left' means the lighthouse is still inside him.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says that 'part of me never really left' the lighthouse. Is there a place from your past — a school, a house, a city — that still feels like it lives inside you? What do you carry from it, even when you are far away?
